_comment: Second-marks measured from the RENDERED video. 0:00 is the video's first
frame — the instant you press Enter to sync the scorer.
One row per word. Each row has three marks:
start   The answer window OPENS. This is where the host begins REVEALING
THE PREVIOUS WORD's answer — not where this word's hint starts.
(For word 1 there is no previous answer: it's where the intro
ends and the host begins the lead-in.) Scoring for the previous
word stops here, which is exactly right: its answer is now spoken
aloud, so nobody can score it by copying the host.
teaser  Where the host speaks the SHORT hint. The overlay puts it on
screen at this second — before it, the lower-third is blank.
hint    Where the host speaks the LONG hint, ~4s later. It's added below
the teaser; the teaser stays up.
So a row runs: [start] reveal previous + lead-in ... [teaser] short hint ...
4s pause ... [hint] long hint ... ~5s to guess ... then the NEXT row's start
closes this window.
Marks may be plain seconds (95) or clock strings ("1:35").
starts must be strictly increasing across rows, and
start <= teaser < hint < (next row's start).
The scorer NEEDS 'start' + 'outro_start'; those drive scoring. 'teaser' and
'hint' only drive the overlay — leave them null and every hint just shows at
window open. A bad mark is printed and ignored, never scored on.
The middle hint in questions.json is a SPARE the host never reads. It is
never shown on screen either — only two marks per row.
